sentences.text: Well, it went by until the beginning of 1942.
And there were-- we knew already that we are going to be next [INAUDIBLE] going to be next to-- to evacuate, you know.
So my brother-- my father prepared us, you know, to put us on the list.
We went-- we went to work every day anyway.
So we went.
We got dressed every morning, double-- double clothes, you know, in case we will-- they-- they take us away, we'll have something to wear anyway.
We went to work one day.
One day, we went to work.
And-- and at work, this messenger, this girl, Polish girl, came on a bike.
She brought a message for my father.
They're surrounding the city.
And-- and this is it.
This is the end.
And by midnight, probably, we'll all have to be out on the-- in the square, you know.
And they're going to keep us [INAUDIBLE] and others.
And go in and see Applebaum and see what he can do to send a car immediately.
And that was around noon time after when I got the message.
So everybody, the whole group, we were a group of 60.
They got-- my brother was there.
And my brother-in-law was working with me together.
So and they went back.
And they got the orders to go back to the city, because they're surrounding the city.
We're got to take the whole group surrounding, too.
Maybe they'll pick him for work, or maybe they'll put him on the transport, you know.
So they had to go back.
I remained-- sneaked away.
And I remained behind him.
I didn't go back with the group.
And I saw from far already, the-- the trucks are going.
The German are back and forth.
And I saw a lot of activities.
So immediately, I run into this Applebaum, to this place they formed.
It's called [INAUDIBLE], you know, in that place.
I happened to be right there.
So I went into him.
And I said, this-- this is the last [INAUDIBLE].
Better do it as fast as you can.
I said, I can't do nothing.
I have no car, and this and that, the whole story.
Give me your passport, I mean, you-- you paper.
I'll take you, and I'll sign you in.
You're going to be working with-- you'll be in our place.
You'll be saved.
I said, what [INAUDIBLE]?
I'm going to be alone.
Anyway, I have no choice.
I said, I don't want me.
I want my-- for my-- for my family.
Anyway, I can't do nothing.
So a whole night, I couldn't go back.
The minute I didn't want to go back, it was 5 miles to walk to a city.
And they warned me, don't go.
Because the minute you get there, they-- they surrounded the front of the city.
They'll shoot you right away if you come in.
So I didn't go.
And the whole night, I couldn't sleep.
And there were some people that comforted me.
I was crying, you know.
I knew already that's the end.
The next day, I-- I walked-- there was right on the railroad train-- the-- the-- Railroad tracks.
--railroad tracks.
And I stood and stood.
And I saw from far, I could see it's 5 miles.
But we could see, there was a little hill.
I could see.
The city was lower.
So I could see the train, the train standing there.
And I didn't see people, but I knew already this is it.
They brought into our city, because it's a train station from the surrounding maybe 10 cities, 10 small towns.
They round them up and ask where, and they load them up at our station.
And around 11 o'clock in the morning, when I'm going to see the train is coming, the smoke is going.
And the Germans from far, see, with the machine guns, they're outside the train.
They're watching.
The trains had very little windows.
And I watched, and I watched.
And I was-- started to cry.
And I said, this is it.
I'm alone.
I didn't know what happened to my brother, or they selected him.
And I see on the train-- but the train was slowing down, because it was a crossing.
And-- and I saw-- apparently my sister must have noticed me standing there.
I was all alone in a field, you know,   near the track.
And I saw a hand sticking out through the window.
And she was wearing a green dress knitted-- she knitted herself.
And she was waving.
And I said, this is my sister.
It must be 20, 30 cars--
--freight cars, and I noticed.
The color attracted me.
And I knew, this is it.
That was it.
And the train was gone.
And that was it.
And I'm left.
And I started to cry.
And I didn't know what to do, what to think, or what to do.
And-- and I looked on the highway.
I spotted a group of people.
It was maybe 2 miles away.
And the highway wasn't far from there.
And the whole, like, chain of people, you know, walking, and they got closer and closer and closer.
And they came-- they-- and our place of work wasn't far from the [INAUDIBLE].
There was a mine we-- we were supposed to work.
We worked that day when the girl came with the message.
So what happened-- so when they got close, I saw already this is our people.
They selected them on the place for them to work in the-- in the mine.
And they selected 60 of them.
I got closer, and I wanted to join.
Except now, I wasn't on the list.
So the manager said, you can't do it, because you're not on the list.
They're going to kill you.
I don't care, I said.
I'll-- I just joined them.
And they chased them down maybe another 5 miles to the mine.
That's going to be the place they're going to work.
And they made a barrack there and all that.
And we walked down.
We-- we didn't-- we walked to the place.
And when we got to the place, there were three Ukrainian officers in green uniforms.

They were supposed to take over, to guard us, like on the-- they put us in one room, a huge barn, you know, with-- they made, like, wooden places to sleep, like-- I don't know what you call it, the double deckers.
We slept maybe.
And the-- and they started to count us when we arrived to the place of work.
They started to check the list, how many boys on the list.
So they started to count us.
And they count up 61.
So-- so everybody was yelling, oh, this is George Kohn.
He came in.
He's the-- he's the 61.
And they said, we have to take out one and shoot.
Who is the one?
So nobody said a word in the meeting.
So something happened.
This was a miracle happened.
A boy just run away.
He escaped from the group.
While they were counting, he escaped.
They got panicky.
So they count again.
So they count again, it balanced.
It became 60.
So, all right.
So, I remained.
I'm alive.
So they put us in a-- it was towards the evening already.
It was dark.
And it was October, you know.
Yeah, October.
Yeah, October.
And it was cold, miserable, rainy.
And they locked us up in the barn from outside.
You couldn't get out, you know.
If a fire, everything is gone.
But nobody was sleeping.
Everyone was crying, you know.
This is a tragedy.
It just-- it just crashed.
It just happened.
And everybody had-- whatever the possession they gave them [INAUDIBLE], every boy had something in their possession.
So while they locked us in, one of the officers comes in from the Ukraine.
He asks, who's a barber?
So I-- I acted first.
I said, I'm a barber.
I'm a barber like I'm a shoemaker.
So we need a barber.
So they had separate quarters, you know.
They stayed, you know, for the-- for the three of them.
So, all right.
You're a barber?
You come with me.
And you're going to stay with us.
You're going to shave us every day, you know.
And they're going to go to work.
And you're going to watch here with us.
You're going-- you shine my shoes.
So that's a good job.
So I figure this is going to be a help for my brother.
For-- I'm going to have extra-- well, they're supposed to give us bread, you know, every day and soup.
That's all.
They-- they told us, you have to work.
We'll give you to eat.
And everything's going to be all right.
So it was a couple of weeks.
It was good for me.
I didn't go to work.
And I-- and I helped-- my kid brother went to work with my brother-in-law.
I always have extra piece of bread for them, you know.
I was hiding, you know.
And one day, they tipped me off, the Ukrainian, that you know tomorrow the SS are coming to check, you know.
They're going to check.
And they're going to probably-- they're coming to check if whatever remains, more people have money or jewelry, whatever, that they told me they got information about.
They're going to come.
And I didn't say anything, so right away, I-- I didn't tell anybody.
I told my kid brother, whatever you have, you better hide it.
Because it's going to be gone.
And I, whatever I had-- we-- we had some money.
But the thing that my father hid, it was still there.
We didn't go back.
So what happened?
So they arrived the next day.
And nobody knew about it.
And it  was a surprise.
I didn't [INAUDIBLE].
They told me not to say.
If you say one word, you get shot.
Don't tell nobody.
When I came in, I'll never forget.
I made a stove to heat up the-- the room when the SS came.
I remember one SS man said, who is this?
He said, he is my assistant.
He works for us.
He said, the Jew-- one of the Jews, you know?
And I made a fire for the stove.
So the-- the German tells me, you make-- make it burn faster.
Because it doesn't burn.
He put my head and in the oven, I should blow.
I'll never forget.
Oh, it was so frightening.
Anyway, let me go.
And I set up tables.
They took everybody out.
And then they didn't go to work.
They said, don't send them to work.
He told the Ukrainian were going to make a search.
They set up tables and the two lines of the [INAUDIBLE] you know.
And I was amongst them, too, you know.
They wanted no exception.
And they start-- they gave a speech.
You are here to work.
We'll give you food.
And you got the shelter.
You got the place where to sleep.
And no more business with you, no more jewelry, no more money.
Whatever you have, put it on the table.
So people got frightened.
Whatever we find anything afterwards, we're going to search, you're going to be shot immediately.
So everybody got panicky.
Everybody-- piles of money and everything-- that was the fresh.
Everybody had hidden stuff.
They were flying from all sides.
And they start the search.
And finally, there was a boy.
I remember.
The face is in front of me.
They searched, and they found some money in the-- in the underwear, you know And immediately, they gave an order to the-- to the Ukrainian.
The German didn't shoot him.
The-- the three of them, they were standing with the rifles.
And they said, in German, [NON-ENGLISH].
They put him in the front of us, this boy, and shot him.
So one could have shot him.
But the Ukrainian, they gave him bullets.
One, two, three-- dead, finished.
And then, while I was in charge, the Ukrainian said, you-- you work for us?
You bury him.
Myself, I had to take a boy from the-- from the group.
And we had to [INAUDIBLE] a little, not far from us.
And we  buried him.
There, finished.
That was it.
But [INAUDIBLE] then the SS went back.
We remained in the same mine, you know.
We worked.
It didn't last long-- a few weeks, maybe.
A few weeks, they came again--
Yeah, yeah.
We were together.
Yeah, he had.
And as a matter of fact, he told me he had a $50 bill stuck here like this, rolled it up.
And-- and he told me where  he had it.
And nobody knew it.
[INAUDIBLE] showed [INAUDIBLE]
A finger.
So fast?
What--
Yeah.
So what happened-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah.
So they came back a few weeks later and said, we not going to-- we're going to liquidate this-- this place.
We're going to have a new place for you.
We're going to take you to the factory where they produce iron ore.
That was the same place where Applebaum remember the-- in Ostrowiec.
They're going to chase us there.
On the way, there were another group came in to take us to the-- to this working place, Ostrowiec.
There was like a ghetto, you know.
And on the way, it must have been maybe 10 miles, we walked.
And on the way, this group they said, oh, for themselves.
They-- they knew that the Jews have money and this and that.
So they set up another-- another gimmick.
You know, we're going to search.
Whoever has money-- you know, they thought we still have.
They still had, you know.
So in the middle in a forest, like, in middle of the way, they stopped us.
And they picked me.
And this is a true story.
They picked me.
And they take another boy, and they gave me two shovels.
And we dug a-- a grave, you know, like a grave or some form of a grave.
And it took maybe an hour to dig this or maybe an hour and a half, not too deep.
And all of a sudden, I'm standing in the grave.
And they told me, don't come up.
We're going to search.
And they told the-- the gang, you know, we're going to search you.
And if you don't give up everything, we're going to find anything, those two are going to be shot on the grave, you know.
What can you do?
Well, it was flying again, money.
And then that, they were satisfied.
And they pulled us out of the grave.
And we survived again.
And we continue.
We went to the-- to the place of work.
We came in there a mess, you know, nothing to eat.
And it was terrible.
And they set us up.
We're going to go to work, like shifts, eight hour shift in the factory.
We had to [INAUDIBLE] maybe 2 miles to the factory from that ghetto.
In that-- in that ghetto was only-- not only us.
There was a lot of people, boys from the other town.
There was a lot of--
It was-- Ostrowiec, Ostrowiec.
We worked there maybe three months or something.
So when I came, we worked in shifts.
My brother went on one shift.
I made a [INAUDIBLE].
I made it with the manager, one should stay home and prepare some food, something.
So he went to one shift, and I went to the other shift.
When I came back-- when he came back from the shift, they took him for another shift, you know.
Sometimes we worked three shifts.
You know, just-- it was terrible.
They were shooting left and right people for not working.
Anyway, it was a miserable place to be.
And they had some Jewish kapos, he called them, you know.
And they-- they were worse than anybody else.
But what can you do?
That was-- that was life.
That's the way it-- it was.
But it didn't last long.
It came, like, I would call a Moses.
It's not Moses.
That was [INAUDIBLE], you know.
[INAUDIBLE] story, [INAUDIBLE].
Came into the ghetto, he was wearing like a uniform.
He was-- he was-- he was a genius of a boy, you know, from our hometown.
He escaped-- he escaped the-- the liquidation, you know.
He escaped.
He was loose.
He had a gun.
Anyway, he found-- he found a German.
He gave him a permit for 20 boys, for 20 people to take out from the ghetto.
They're going to form a place in my hometown, where original they liquidated.
They're going to take him to his brother's house.
And there we're going to have like a fiction-- like a fixed place, you know.
We're going to work.
It wasn't true, but it was done for money, you know.
He had plenty of money.
He comes in to the ghetto.
And picks-- whom does he pick?
He knows already whom to pick.
We were 60.
He selected 20.
He knew who had money.
We had to pay for the place.
So naturally, it was my brother and myself and my brother-in-law, we were the first three.
We knew the money's there.
And then he selected some more with money and lined us up.
And-- and he gave us-- they give us permission.
He had a permit from the SS, go back to the city.
And we walked back 10 kilometers.
And it was a joy for us.
We're going back to our hometown.
Nobody was home already.
It was-- this was a few months later.
So we got into this house.
And they set us up there.
We had where to sleep.
It was a big house.
And food, I don't remember what we-- no, we-- we were home already.
I mean, the gentile really helped us, you know.
And we didn't do-- we didn't go to-- we-- we went to the mine just to for-- to show that we work, you know.
But it didn't last long.
And then a few boys got sick and typhoid fever in the same place.
And we isolated them in a separate little house that was there.
We didn't want to keep them.
It was very contagious, this.
So nobody wanted to go into this.
We had to give him water.
We had to help them.
But I was the one that went in.
And apparently, I got it from them, you know.
I'll tell you.
I'll get to the story after.
So what happened?
Well, this is a continuation.
Otherwise, I wouldn't have enough time, you know.
What happened is around that-- it was meant to be-- it wasn't-- wasn't meant to be good.
We thought for sure, who knows, it may last for years.
It was only 1942.
So then I decided while being there, I told my kid brother, I said, you know what?
We have to go and get out the stuff that father hid.
Remember?
He didn't know about it.
I say-- I wanted to send him.
He was young.
He was a-- like a kid.
There would be-- there was nobody in the house.
And there was no light.
I gave him a candle.
And I explained to him where it is.
And then I decided to go myself.
And I called this-- [INAUDIBLE] this Mr.
Wenzel, which my father met him when they-- when they evacuate him, he was standing right there.
And he promised my father, I'm going to look after the boys, whatever it will take.
And the minute he found out we in the city, he came right away with soup and everything.
And he helped us a lot.
And I figured, I'm going to call and take him.
And we'll go at night.
We'll take what get out from the house whatever we can.
The house, the doors were open.
Everything was gone.
No, they were starting to-- to take out the-- Polish people, they empty the house, you know, whatever was opened.
So it was at late at night.
It must be around 11 o'clock.
And the SS was stationed right across our house.
And they walked, you know, like-- the-- the guard-- guard walking back and forth.
But that was in the back, you know.
We went in through the back in the middle of the night.
It was 11 o'clock.
And my kid brother went into the basement.
And he knew exactly where it was.
And he came out.
And I saw that he had it under his coat.
And I went up with Wenzel.
We picked up bags of shoes.
And we took it from-- from the back of the street, was a-- a back alley.
We went and took everything.
Wenzel took everything home to his home.
The shoes, must have been 10 bags of shoes.
It took maybe two hours.
And we were lucky.
We got away with it.
Nobody noticed.
We had to be very quietly, you know.
And I had this sole possession.
And  I checked.
It was my father's gold watch.
And there was a lot of-- a lot of valuable things.
So I divided this between us.
I says, in case something, somebody, everybody should have something, you know.
And we went back.
We were very lucky we did with this campaign.
It was a campaign.
And we went back to the-- to the house.
I didn't last long.
Maybe a week later, they surrounded the place.
And they took us down to the train station to load barracks and this.
And they take to the train station, I thought for sure this is it.
We're going to go the same place, another camp.
Who knows?
We saw a freight train loading and this and that.
But it wasn't so.
We were loading barracks, unassembled barracks, you know.
It was a lot of work.
And I was standing on a ramp.
And boys were loading down below.
And down below, maybe six SS men were watching us, you know, and machine guns.
And I had an idea right away in my head, something is  going to-- it's going to give.
Because I'm not going to the concentration, to the gas chambers now.
We have to run.
I can take over my kid brother and my brother-in-law.
And I have meeting, nobody should hear.
And I gave them some money each, you know.
I was in charge and said, here.
And we're going to run.
So my brother-in-law said, not me.
I don't run.
So my kid brother, he was so like a-- like a devil, you know.
And the minute I said-- I gave him the money.
And I watched the SS.
They brought a bottle of liquor.
You know, they were drinking.
And they were eating.
They-- they didn't pay attention to what's going on here.
I said, now is the time.
Look, now is the time.
The minute I said the time, my kid brother is gone, finished.
But remember, I'll meet you at the farm, there and there.
If I-- if I'll succeed to run.
The minute he ran, he disappeared like a shadow.
I didn't see him anymore.
He left.
He went to the left.
And I went to the right on the ramp.
So I said, there's my chance, my turn.
What-- the kid is going to be alone?
What am I going to do?
So I have to do it.
And I didn't tell nobody.
My brother-in-law knew about it.
But they watched me.
I jumped down the-- the ramp.
I watched when they enjoyed themselves.
They were busy.
And there was maybe five sets of tracks, the railway, you know, railway tracks.
And I run across the tracks, lucky I didn't trip.
And I made it very quickly.
And I was touching if I am not shot, you know.
I just hear a shot.
As a matter of fact, right after me, a boy saw me.
One of the boys saw me running.
He--- maybe, maybe 10 minutes later, the-- the German were already loose, you know.
They noticed, and they shot him on the spot, same place where I was.
But I ran right across.
There was a little hill up, I remember.
I went-- there was houses in there across the railway station.
So I went behind the houses.
So I knew already I'm safe.
And then there was a big field across.
My brother left.
He run on the left.
And I went-- and I went right, to the right side.
On the right side, there was the forest.
And I figured, I can't go through the city, because anybody will see me.
They-- they-- everybody knew me in the city, The.
Polish, my friends, schoolboys.
I'll get caught right away.
So I went through the fields.
We had a river going through our city, you know, maybe a mile away.
So I'll ran to the river.
And I knew that river one spot, we used to go in the summer to swim, you know.
This one spot is very shallow.
It was October.
It was cold.
So I went in up till here and waded across the river.
I had to cross the river in order to get to the forest, you know.
And I went with the forest in the middle of the night.
I knew the forest well.
And the place to the farm, it must be maybe-- it was 3 miles, you know.
I walked to the forest, and then I went back to the highway from the forest.
And I knew the village where-- where the farmer-- he was a good friend of my father.
He also had our shoes and our possessions.
Rokita.
It's marked there, Rokita.
That was his name.
He used to be in the United States, and then he came back.
[INAUDIBLE] He did business with my father.
He was a very good friend  of ours.
Gentile, sure.
And-- and my-- when I-- when I got to his house, it was after midnight already.
The dogs were barking.
It was very scary.
And he knew already.
He expected me.
I didn't know if I would come.
My brother told him.
My brother may come.
He waited out.
He waited out.
And finally, he noticed me.
He opened the door for us and grabbed me.
He took me up.
Yeah.
He took me upstairs in the-- in the barn, you know, in the hay, you know.
And he showed me, there's-- there's your brother.
Well, you know.
